schools.summary: Gill: these words which follow are commonly called the decalogue, or ten commands, a system or body of laws selected and adapted to the case of the people of Israel; the witnesses note that God spake all these words, the great law about to be given — the solemn opening of the giving of the Ten Commandments, spoken by God himself (the law coming from the mouth of God: 'And God spake all these words, saying'; the author, 'God spake,' that these laws were uttered by God himself, with his own voice, from the midst of the fire and the cloud at Sinai, not delivered by Moses only nor by an angel, the dignity of the giver marking the weight of the law; the fulness, 'all these words,' the whole Decalogue, the ten commands, a complete body of laws, the sum of the moral law; the abiding character of this law the commentators draw, that it is the eternal rule of righteousness, binding on all men, summed up by Christ in love to God and neighbour; the awful solemnity of God's own voice proclaiming his law to the assembled people; that the law's coming from God himself binds the conscience to obey).

themes: And God spake all these words
saying
God himself speaking all the words of the Decalogue with his own voice
the eternal moral law, summed by Christ in love to God and neighbour, binding all to obey

schools.summary: Gill: this verse does not contain the first of the commands but is a preface to them, showing that God had a right to enact and enjoin the people of Israel laws; the witnesses note God prefacing the law with his name, the LORD their God, who had brought them out of Egypt and the house of bondage — the foundation laid for the law, God's authority and his redeeming love (God first declaring who he is before he commands: 'I am the LORD thy God, which have brought thee out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage'; that this is a preface to the commands, not itself the first, showing that God had a right to enact and enjoin laws on Israel; the ground of his authority, 'I am the LORD thy God,' that as Jehovah, the self-existent and almighty God, and their covenant God, he had the right to command and they the duty to obey; the ground of his claim on their love and obedience, 'which have brought thee out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage,' that he had redeemed them from cruel slavery, and so by the right of redemption they were his, bound to serve him; the gospel order the commentators mark, that redemption goes before the law, mercy before duty, the redeemed obeying out of gratitude; that God's people obey not to be saved but because they are saved).

debates.question: What is the significance of the preface to the Ten Commandments?

sides.position: It grounds the law in God's authority (Jehovah, their covenant God) and his redeeming love (having brought them out of bondage) — redemption going before the law, mercy before duty; the redeemed obey out of gratitude, not to be saved but because they are saved

themes: I am the LORD thy God
which have brought thee out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage
the preface grounding the law in God's authority and redeeming love
redemption going before the law, the redeemed obeying out of gratitude

schools.summary: Gill: this is the first command, opposed to the polytheism of the Gentiles, the Egyptians from whom Israel was just come, requiring that God alone be acknowledged and worshipped; the witnesses note the first commandment forbidding any other gods before the LORD — the foundation of all true religion, that God alone is to be owned and worshipped (the first command claiming God's sole right to be God: 'Thou shalt have no other gods before me'; the matter of the command, that it forbids all other gods, all the false deities of the heathen, especially the gods of Egypt just left and of Canaan, and requires that the LORD alone be acknowledged as God; the force of 'before me,' or before my face, that to set up any other god is done in God's very sight and presence, an affront to him who sees all; the breadth of the command the commentators draw, that it forbids not only gross idolatry but all that puts anything in God's place in the heart, whether self, the world, riches, or pleasure, every idol of the soul; that this first command is the foundation of all the rest, for he who truly has the LORD for his God will keep all his commandments; that God will share his glory and worship with no other).

themes: Thou shalt have no other gods
before me
the first commandment, that God alone is to be owned and worshipped
forbidding not only gross idolatry but every idol of the heart, done before God's very face

schools.summary: Gill: thou shalt not make any image of anything graven by art or man's device, cut out of wood or stone, or anything molten, or cast into a mould; the witnesses note the second commandment forbidding any graven image or likeness of things in heaven, earth, or sea — the prohibition of all images in the worship of God (the second command guarding the manner of God's worship: 'Thou shalt not make unto thee any graven image, or any likeness of any thing that is in heaven above, or that is in the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth'; the breadth of the prohibition, 'any graven image, or any likeness of any thing,' that no image at all, whether graven, carved of wood or stone, or molten, cast of metal, of any creature in heaven, in the earth, in the water, might be made to represent or worship God; the reason the commentators give, that God, being a Spirit, infinite and invisible, cannot be likened to any creature, and to make an image of him is to dishonour and debase him; that the command forbids both the making of idols of false gods and, especially, the worshipping of the true God by images, the corrupting of his worship; that God will be worshipped as he has appointed, not after the inventions of men).

themes: Thou shalt not make unto thee any graven image, or any likeness of any thing that is in heaven above
or that is in the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth
the second commandment forbidding images in the worship of God
God, being a Spirit, cannot be likened to any creature, his worship not to be corrupted by images

schools.summary: Gill: thou shalt not perform any worship to them, show any reverence by any gesture of the body, bowing being put for all other acts of worship; the witnesses note God forbidding to bow down to or serve images, for he is a jealous God, visiting the fathers' iniquity on the children to the third and fourth generation of them that hate him — the prohibition of idolatrous worship and the awful reason of God's jealousy (God guarding his worship with a solemn threat: 'Thou shalt not bow down thyself to them, nor serve them: for I the LORD thy God am a jealous God'; the act forbidden, 'bow down thyself to them, nor serve them,' that no worship at all, neither outward gesture nor inward service, might be given to images; the reason, 'for I the LORD thy God am a jealous God,' that God, like a husband jealous for his wife's love, will not endure a rival in the worship and devotion of his people but is jealous for his own honour; the threat, 'visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children... of them that hate me,' that God would punish idolatry, that great sin and hatred of him, even visiting its effects on the children of the guilty who followed their fathers' sin, to the third and fourth generation; that idolatry is hatred of God, the breaking of the marriage-bond between him and his people).

themes: Thou shalt not bow down thyself to them, nor serve them: for I the LORD thy God am a jealous God
visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children... of them that hate me
God forbidding all worship of images, jealous for his honour
idolatry being hatred of God, the breaking of the marriage-bond between him and his people

schools.summary: Gill: God shows mercy to thousands of them that love him, and show their love by worshipping God, and him only, serving him acceptably with reverence and godly fear, by a cheerful obedience; the witnesses note God promising to show mercy to thousands of them that love him and keep his commandments — the gracious counterpart to the threat, the abundant mercy promised to the obedient (against the threat to idolaters God setting a far greater promise to his lovers: 'And shewing mercy unto thousands of them that love me, and keep my commandments'; the contrast the commentators mark, that the threat reached but to the third and fourth generation of haters, but the mercy to thousands of generations of lovers, God's mercy far outrunning his judgment; the persons, 'them that love me, and keep my commandments,' that true love to God is proved by keeping his commandments, as Christ said, 'If ye love me, keep my commandments,' love and obedience joined; the abundance, 'shewing mercy unto thousands,' that God delights to show mercy, and that to a vast multitude and through long generations, to all who love and obey him; the comfort, that the obedient and their seed are heirs of God's covenant mercy).

themes: And shewing mercy unto thousands
of them that love me, and keep my commandments
God's mercy to thousands of generations, far outrunning the threat to the few
true love proved by keeping his commandments, love and obedience joined

schools.summary: Gill: thou shalt not make use of the name Lord or God, or any other name of the divine Being, in a light and trifling way, without any show of reverence; the name of God ought never to be mentioned but with reverence; the witnesses note the third commandment forbidding to take God's name in vain, with the threat that he will not hold the offender guiltless — the guarding of the holiness and reverence due to God's name (the third command requiring that God's name be hallowed: 'Thou shalt not take the name of the LORD thy God in vain; for the LORD will not hold him guiltless that taketh his name in vain'; the breadth of the sin forbidden, 'take the name of the LORD... in vain,' that it is to use the name, titles, or word of God in a light and trifling way without any show of reverence, whether in idle speech, false or rash oaths, perjury, cursing, profane jesting, or hypocritical worship; the reason it is so grave, that the name of God ought never to be mentioned but with reverence, it being holy and to be hallowed; the solemn threat, 'the LORD will not hold him guiltless,' that, though men make light of this sin and think it small, God will surely punish it, accounting the profaner guilty; that the reverence of God's name is the reverence of God himself).

themes: Thou shalt not take the name of the LORD thy God in vain
for the LORD will not hold him guiltless that taketh his name in vain
the third commandment forbidding the misuse of God's holy name
though men make light of this sin, God will not hold the profaner guiltless

schools.summary: Gill: to keep the sabbath holy by abstaining from all servile work and business, and from all pleasures and recreations lawful on other days, and by spending it in religious exercises, both internal and external; the witnesses note the fourth commandment, to remember the sabbath day and keep it holy — the setting apart of a day of holy rest and worship (God claiming a day as holy to himself: 'Remember the sabbath day, to keep it holy'; the word 'Remember,' that the sabbath was not a wholly new thing, it was known from the creation and the giving of the manna, and that they should bear it in mind beforehand, preparing for it, and not forget it; the duty, 'to keep it holy,' that the day should be hallowed, set apart from common use, by abstaining from all servile work and business, and from lawful recreations, and by spending it in religious exercises, both internal and external; the sanctifying of the day, that it is to be kept holy to the LORD, a day of rest and worship; the abiding obligation the commentators draw, that a sabbath of rest and worship is a perpetual ordinance, the Lord's day under the gospel kept in its room; that God is to be served in his own appointed times).

themes: Remember the sabbath day
to keep it holy
the fourth commandment, to set apart a day of holy rest and worship
a perpetual ordinance, the Lord's day kept in its room under the gospel

schools.summary: Gill: this is not to be taken for a precept but a permission, a grant and allowance to work and provide for oneself and family the six days, that the seventh might be kept holy; the witnesses note the granting of six days for labour and all the week's work — the appointing of the working week, that the seventh might be reserved for God (the command marking off the six days from the holy seventh: 'Six days shalt thou labour, and do all thy work'; that this is not a precept but a permission, not a command that men must always be at work, but a grant and allowance that the six days are given them to labour and provide for themselves and their families things useful and necessary, and honest in the sight of God; the limit implied, 'do all thy work' in the six days, that the ordinary business of life should be done within the working week, so that nothing be left to encroach on the sabbath; the wisdom and goodness of God in this division, giving men ample time for their needful labour, yet reserving a seventh part for himself; that diligent and honest labour in its season is itself acceptable to God).

themes: Six days shalt thou labour
and do all thy work
the six days granted for honest labour, a permission not a command
the week's work done within the six days, nothing to encroach on the sabbath

schools.summary: Gill: the seventh day is the sabbath of the Lord thy God, a day set apart for his service, on which no work was to be done; the witnesses note the seventh day being the sabbath of the LORD, on which neither the man, nor his children, servants, cattle, nor stranger should do any work — the rest claimed for all within the gates, the day belonging to God (the seventh day wholly God's: 'the seventh day is the sabbath of the LORD thy God: in it thou shalt not do any work, thou, nor thy son, nor thy daughter... nor thy stranger that is within thy gates'; whose day it is, 'the sabbath of the LORD thy God,' that the seventh day is set apart for the LORD, his own day, to be kept holy to him; the rest commanded, 'in it thou shalt not do any work,' that all servile labour was to cease; the breadth of the rest, reaching to thy son, daughter, manservant, maidservant, cattle, stranger, that not the master only but all the household, even the servants, the beasts, and the foreigner within the gates, should rest, a mercy to all; the equity and kindness of the law, that even servants and beasts should have their rest; that the day is to be wholly given to God).

themes: the seventh day is the sabbath of the LORD thy God: in it thou shalt not do any work
nor thy stranger that is within thy gates
the seventh day the LORD's, a rest for all the household
the equity and kindness of the law, that even servants and beasts should have their rest

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: it is not improbable that the work of creation was made to occupy six days because one day in seven is the appropriate proportion of rest to labour for man; the witnesses note the reason of the sabbath given, that in six days the LORD made all things and rested the seventh, blessing and hallowing the day — the grounding of the sabbath in God's own pattern of creation and rest (the sabbath resting on God's example: 'in six days the LORD made heaven and earth... and rested the seventh day: wherefore the LORD blessed the sabbath day, and hallowed it'; the reason, 'in six days the LORD made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that in them is,' that God made the whole creation in six days and rested the seventh, not from weariness but ceasing from the work of creation; the consequence, 'wherefore the LORD blessed the sabbath day, and hallowed it,' that, in memory of this, God set apart and sanctified the seventh day from the beginning, making it a blessed and holy day; that the sabbath is thus founded not on Israel's law only but on the creation, a memorial of God's making and resting, binding from the first; Ellicott's thought that one day in seven is the fitting proportion of rest to labour for man; that the rest of God is the pattern and pledge of the rest of his people).

debates.question: On what is the sabbath founded?

sides.position: On the creation itself — God made all things in six days and rested the seventh, then blessed and hallowed the day from the beginning (Genesis 2:3); the sabbath is founded not on Israel's law only but on God's own pattern of work and rest, binding from the first, the rest of God the pattern and pledge of the rest of his people

themes: in six days the LORD made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that in them is, and rested the seventh day
wherefore the LORD blessed the sabbath day, and hallowed it
the sabbath grounded in God's own pattern of creation and rest
founded not on Israel's law only but on the creation, the rest of God the pattern of his people's rest

schools.summary: Gill: this is the fifth commandment of the decalogue, but the first commandment with promise, as the apostle says, and the first of the second table; the witnesses note the fifth commandment, to honour father and mother, with the promise of long days in the land — the first commandment of the second table, with its promise of blessing (God beginning the duties to men with honour to parents: 'Honour thy father and thy mother: that thy days may be long upon the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee'; the place of this command, the first of the second table, the duties to our neighbour, and the first commandment with promise, as Paul notes; the duty, 'Honour thy father and thy mother,' that children should reverence, love, obey, and in their need maintain their parents, and that this honour extends to all set over us, in family, church, and state; the promise annexed, 'that thy days may be long upon the land,' that long life and prosperity in the land were promised to the obedient and dutiful, a special blessing on this duty; that God so values the honour of parents that he sets his promise of blessing upon it; that the keeping of this command is the well-being of families and of society).

themes: Honour thy father and thy mother
that thy days may be long upon the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee
the fifth commandment, the first of the second table and the first with promise
the duty extending to all set over us, with long life promised to the dutiful

schools.summary: Gill: this means not any sort of creatures, for some are to be killed for food and for safety, but rational creatures, men, women, and children, any of the human species; the unjust taking away of human life is forbidden; the witnesses note the sixth commandment forbidding the killing of man — the guarding of human life, made in God's image (the command protecting the life of man: 'Thou shalt not kill'; the meaning, that it forbids not any sort of creatures, for beasts may be killed for food and men defended, but the unjust taking away of rational creatures, men, women, and children, any human life; the ground, that man is made in the image of God, so that to shed man's blood is to assault God's image; the breadth Christ gave the command, that it forbids not only the act of murder but also unjust anger, hatred, and malice in the heart, which are the seeds of murder, whosoever hateth his brother is a murderer; that it also requires the preserving of life, our own and our neighbour's; that the law reaches the heart, condemning the inward as well as the outward sin).

debates.question: How far does the sixth commandment reach?

sides.position: Beyond the outward act of murder to unjust anger, hatred, and malice in the heart — which Christ taught are the seeds of murder ('whosoever hateth his brother is a murderer,' 1 John 3:15) — and it also requires the preserving of life; the law reaches the heart

themes: Thou shalt not
kill
the sixth commandment forbidding the unjust taking of human life, made in God's image
Christ extending it to anger and hatred in the heart, the seeds of murder

schools.summary: Gill: adultery, strictly, is the sin committed with another man's wife, but the word is taken to signify whoredom and fornication also; all unlawful lusts and uncleanness are forbidden; the witnesses note the seventh commandment forbidding adultery, and under it all uncleanness — the guarding of the marriage-bond and of chastity (the command protecting purity and the marriage-tie: 'Thou shalt not commit adultery'; the strict sense, that adultery is the violation of the marriage-bond, the sin with another man's wife; the wider reach, that, though the word strictly means adultery, it is taken to forbid whoredom and fornication and all unlawful lusts and uncleanness, every breach of chastity; the breadth Christ gave it, that it forbids not the outward act only but the lustful look and desire, whosoever looketh on a woman to lust after her hath committed adultery with her already in his heart; that the command guards the holiness of marriage and the purity of body and soul, requiring chastity in heart, speech, and behaviour; that the law reaches the inward desire as well as the outward deed).

themes: Thou shalt not
commit adultery
the seventh commandment forbidding adultery and all uncleanness
Christ extending it to the lustful look and desire, guarding the purity of body and soul

schools.summary: Gill: to steal is to take away another man's property by force or fraud, without the knowledge and against the will of the owner; thefts are of various kinds, private and public; the witnesses note the eighth commandment forbidding theft, the taking of what is another's — the guarding of every man's property and right (the command protecting what is justly our neighbour's: 'Thou shalt not steal'; the meaning, that to steal is to take away another man's property by force or fraud, without the knowledge and against the will of the owner; the many kinds of theft the commentators name, private stealing, robbery, fraud, false weights and measures, oppression, withholding wages or debts, and every unjust gaining of another's goods; that the command forbids all unrighteous getting and keeping of what is not ours, and requires honesty, justice, and the rendering to every man his own; that it also requires industry and lawful labour, and the relieving of the poor, that we may have to give rather than take; that the law guards the eighth commandment against the inward covetousness that breeds theft).

themes: Thou shalt not
steal
the eighth commandment forbidding theft in all its kinds
requiring honesty, justice, industry, and the rendering to every man his own

schools.summary: Gill: neither publicly in a court, by laying false things to his charge and swearing to them, to his hurt; nor privately, by whispering, tale-bearing, backbiting, and slandering; the witnesses note the ninth commandment forbidding false witness against the neighbour — the guarding of truth and of the neighbour's good name (the command protecting truth and reputation: 'Thou shalt not bear false witness against thy neighbour'; the public sense, that it forbids bearing false witness in a court of judicature, laying false charges and swearing to them, to his hurt and damage, perjury that wrongs the innocent; the private sense, that it forbids also whispering, tale-bearing, backbiting, slandering, and all lying that injures a neighbour's good name; that the command forbids all falsehood and untruth in our dealings and speech, and requires truth-speaking, the maintaining of our neighbour's good name, and a charitable judgment of him; that God, who is the God of truth, hates lying lips, and that a good name is a precious thing not to be wronged; that the law guards both the tongue and the heart from all that is false).

themes: Thou shalt not bear false witness
against thy neighbour
the ninth commandment forbidding false witness in court and in private
requiring truth-speaking and the maintaining of the neighbour's good name, for God hates lying lips

schools.summary: Gill: this is the tenth and last commandment, an explanation of several of the past, showing that the law of God forbids not only external acts of sin but the inward and first motions of the mind to it; the witnesses note the tenth commandment forbidding to covet the neighbour's house, wife, servants, goods, or anything that is his — the reaching of the law to the very heart and its first desires (the command going inmost, forbidding the lust itself: 'Thou shalt not covet thy neighbour's house... nor any thing that is thy neighbour's'; the breadth of the things named, house, wife, manservant, maidservant, ox, ass, any thing that is thy neighbour's, that all that belongs to another is fenced from our desire; the great matter the commentators draw, that this command is an explanation of several of the past, showing that the law of God not only forbids external acts of sin but the inward and first motions of the mind to it, the secret lust and desire, before any deed; that this is the command by which Paul was convinced of sin, I had not known lust except the law had said, Thou shalt not covet, for it searches the heart; that the law is spiritual, reaching the inmost desires, and that no man can keep it perfectly, so that it shuts up all to the grace of Christ).

debates.question: What does the tenth commandment ('Thou shalt not covet') reveal about the law?

sides.position: That the law is spiritual, forbidding not only external acts but the inward and first motions of the mind — the secret lust and desire, before any deed; it was the command by which Paul was convinced of sin (Romans 7:7), and since no man keeps it perfectly, it shuts up all to the grace of Christ

themes: Thou shalt not covet thy neighbour's house, thou shalt not covet thy neighbour's wife
nor any thing that is thy neighbour's
the tenth commandment forbidding the very coveting of the heart
the law spiritual, reaching the inmost desires, the command by which Paul was convinced of sin

schools.summary: Gill: they heard the dreadful volleys of thunder and saw the amazing flashes of lightning, which were like lamps and torches; the witnesses note all the people perceiving the thunderings, lightnings, trumpet, and smoking mountain, and removing to stand afar off — the terror of the people at the awful signs of God's presence on the mount (the giving of the law attended with dreadful majesty: 'all the people saw the thunderings, and the lightnings... and the mountain smoking: and when the people saw it, they removed, and stood afar off'; the awful signs, thunderings, lightnings, noise of the trumpet, the mountain smoking, the terrible tokens of God's presence on Sinai, the thunder, the flashing lightning, the trumpet ever louder, and the mountain wrapped in smoke and fire; the effect, that the people saw it, heard and beheld the dreadful display, and removed and stood afar off, shrinking back in terror from the holy mount; that the law was given with such majesty and terror to impress on the people the holiness and greatness of the Lawgiver, and the dreadfulness of his justice against sin; the contrast the commentators draw with the gospel, that we are come not to the mount that burned but to Mount Zion).

themes: all the people saw the thunderings, and the lightnings, and the noise of the trumpet, and the mountain smoking
they removed, and stood afar off
the people terrified by the awful signs of God's presence on Sinai
the law given with majesty to impress the holiness of the Lawgiver, a contrast with the gospel's Mount Zion

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the people asked Moses to speak with them, but that God might not speak with them, lest they die; the witnesses note the people asking Moses to speak with them and they would hear, but begging that God not speak directly, lest they die — the people's plea for a mediator, conscious they could not bear God's immediate voice (the terror driving them to seek a go-between: 'Speak thou with us, and we will hear: but let not God speak with us, lest we die'; the request, 'Speak thou with us, and we will hear,' that they would have Moses as a go-between, willing to hear God's word through him; the dread, 'let not God speak with us, lest we die,' that they felt they could not bear the immediate voice of the holy God and feared death if he spoke to them directly; the type the commentators draw, that here Moses is a figure of Christ the Mediator, between God and his people, through whom alone sinful men may safely draw near to God; that the people, terrified by the law, felt their need of a mediator, as all sinners do; that man, since the fall, cannot endure the unveiled presence of God but must come by a Mediator).

debates.question: What does the people's plea for Moses to mediate prefigure?

sides.position: Christ the Mediator — Moses here a figure of the one Mediator between God and men, through whom alone sinful men may safely draw near to God; the people, unable to bear God's immediate voice, felt their need of a mediator, as all sinners do

themes: Speak thou with us, and we will hear
but let not God speak with us, lest we die
the people's plea for a mediator, unable to bear God's immediate voice
Moses a figure of Christ the Mediator, through whom alone sinful men may draw near to God

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the maxim, 'Fear not: for God is come to prove you, and that his fear may be before your faces,' that a right fear of God keeps from sin; the witnesses note Moses telling the people not to fear, for God had come to prove them, and that his fear might be before them, that they sin not — the distinguishing of the two fears, the terror to be put away and the reverence to be kept (Moses correcting their fear while pressing a better: 'Fear not: for God is come to prove you, and that his fear may be before your faces, that ye sin not'; the comfort, 'Fear not,' that they need not be in a slavish terror of death, for God meant them no harm; the purpose of the awful display, 'God is come to prove you,' to test and try them, whether they would obey, and to make a deep impression on them; the better fear pressed, 'that his fear may be before your faces, that ye sin not,' that, though they should put away the slavish dread, they should keep the reverent, filial fear of God ever before them, which keeps from sin; the great distinction the commentators draw, between the fear that hath torment, to be cast out, and the reverent fear of God, to be kept, the one driving from God, the other keeping from sin; that the right fear of God is the guard of obedience).

debates.question: How does Moses distinguish two kinds of fear?

sides.position: The slavish terror of death is to be put away ('Fear not'), but the reverent, filial fear of God is to be kept ever before them, which keeps from sin — the one driving from God, the other guarding obedience

themes: Fear not: for God is come to prove you
and that his fear may be before your faces, that ye sin not
Moses distinguishing the slavish terror to be put away from the reverent fear to be kept
the reverent fear of God the guard of obedience, which keeps from sin

schools.summary: Gill: the people kept their distance in their camp and tents, while Moses approached the thick darkness where God was; the witnesses note the people keeping afar off, while Moses alone drew near to the thick darkness where God was — the contrast of the trembling people and the mediator drawing near to God (Moses going in where the people dare not: 'the people stood afar off, and Moses drew near unto the thick darkness where God was'; the people's place, 'the people stood afar off,' that, in their terror, they kept their distance, not daring to approach the holy mount; the mediator's boldness, 'Moses drew near unto the thick darkness where God was,' that Moses, as the appointed mediator, went up alone into the thick darkness, the cloud of God's presence, where the people could not come; the type the commentators draw, of Christ the Mediator, who alone enters into the immediate presence of God for his people, into the holiest, where they cannot of themselves come; that the thick darkness was the veil of God's majesty, in which he dwelt unseen; that through the Mediator the people had access, though they stood afar).

themes: And the people stood afar off
and Moses drew near unto the thick darkness where God was
the trembling people afar while the mediator drew near to God
a type of Christ the Mediator, who alone enters God's presence for his people

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the LORD said unto Moses, Thus thou shalt say unto the children of Israel, Ye have seen that I have talked with you from heaven; the witnesses note God bidding Moses tell Israel that they had seen God talk with them from heaven — the witness of God's own speaking, the ground of the worship-laws to follow (God appealing to what they had heard: 'Thus thou shalt say unto the children of Israel, Ye have seen that I have talked with you from heaven'; the appeal, 'Ye have seen that I have talked with you from heaven,' that the people had themselves been witnesses of God's speaking the law to them, his voice coming from heaven, from the cloud and fire on the mount; the force of this for what follows, that, since God himself had so plainly spoken to them, they were bound to receive and keep his commands and to worship him aright; that the certainty of the law's divine origin, attested by their own eyes and ears, left them without excuse; that this is laid as the ground of the laws of worship now to be given; that God's having spoken binds the conscience to obey).

themes: Thus thou shalt say unto the children of Israel
Ye have seen that I have talked with you from heaven
God appealing to what the people themselves witnessed, his speaking from heaven
the law's divine origin attested by their own eyes and ears, binding them to obey

schools.summary: Gill: 'Ye shall not make with me' is a proposition of itself, and 'gods of silver' belongs to the next clause; that they should not make any gods, especially of silver or gold, to be worshipped with or beside the true God; the witnesses note God forbidding them to make gods of silver or gold to set beside him — the pressing again of the first and second commandments, against all idols (God, having spoken from heaven, forbidding all rivals: 'Ye shall not make with me gods of silver, neither shall ye make unto you gods of gold'; the force of 'with me,' that they should make no god with or beside the true God, who had just spoken to them, to be worshipped along with him or in his stead; the things forbidden, 'gods of silver... gods of gold,' the costly idols of the heathen, of precious metal, which men prize and trust; that this presses again the substance of the first two commandments, that God alone is to be worshipped, and by no image, especially after he had so plainly revealed himself by his voice; the fitness, that, having heard God speak, they should the less be tempted to seek a visible image, the voice from heaven being witness enough of the unseen God; that God will have no rival, however costly).

themes: Ye shall not make with me gods of silver
neither shall ye make unto you gods of gold
God forbidding all gods of silver or gold beside himself
pressing again the first two commandments, for God will have no rival, however costly

schools.summary: Gill: this was a temporary precept, in force until the tabernacle was built, respecting occasional altars made of turfs of earth, easily and quickly made; the witnesses note God commanding an altar of earth for their sacrifices, and promising to come and bless them in every place where he records his name — the simple altar and the gracious promise of God's presence in worship (God appointing a plain altar and binding his blessing to his appointed worship: 'An altar of earth thou shalt make unto me... in all places where I record my name I will come unto thee, and I will bless thee'; the simplicity required, 'an altar of earth,' a plain altar of turf or earth, not costly or carved, a temporary precept for the time before the tabernacle, marking that God seeks not outward show but true worship; the sacrifices, 'thy burnt offerings, and thy peace offerings,' the worship to be offered on it; the great promise, 'in all places where I record my name I will come unto thee, and I will bless thee,' that wherever God appoints his worship and puts his name, he will graciously meet his people and bless them; the comfort the commentators draw, that God meets and blesses his people in his own appointed worship, his presence and blessing the great reward of true worshippers; that God is found where he has promised to be found).

themes: An altar of earth thou shalt make unto me, and shalt sacrifice thereon thy burnt offerings, and thy peace offerings
in all places where I record my name I will come unto thee, and I will bless thee
God appointing a plain altar, seeking not outward show but true worship
God meeting and blessing his people in his own appointed worship, where he records his name

schools.summary: Gill: if they chose instead of an earthen altar to make one of stone, as they might in rocky places, it was to be of unhewn stone; the witnesses note God allowing an altar of stone, but forbidding hewn stone, for lifting a tool upon it would pollute it — the requiring of plainness and the forbidding of man's art in God's altar (God having his altar simple, not shaped by the chisel: 'if thou wilt make me an altar of stone, thou shalt not build it of hewn stone: for if thou lift up thy tool upon it, thou hast polluted it'; the permission, 'if thou wilt make me an altar of stone,' that in rocky places they might use stone instead of earth; the restriction, 'thou shalt not build it of hewn stone,' that the stones must be whole and unhewn, as God found them, not shaped by man's tools; the reason, 'if thou lift up thy tool upon it, thou hast polluted it,' that to carve or dress the stone with a tool would pollute the altar, marring it with man's workmanship; the lessons the commentators draw, that God seeks no ornament of human art in his worship, lest men glory in their own work, and that his worship is to be kept pure and simple, after his own appointment, not adorned by the inventions of men; that man's additions to God's worship corrupt it).

themes: if thou wilt make me an altar of stone, thou shalt not build it of hewn stone
for if thou lift up thy tool upon it, thou hast polluted it
God requiring plainness, the altar of unhewn stone not shaped by man's tool
God seeking no ornament of human art in his worship, lest men glory in their own work

schools.summary: Gill: neither shall the priests, the sons of Aaron, go up by steps to the altar, lest in so doing their nakedness be uncovered; the witnesses note God forbidding the priests to go up to his altar by steps, lest their nakedness be uncovered — the guarding of decency and modesty in the worship of God (God having all done decently in his service: 'Neither shalt thou go up by steps unto mine altar, that thy nakedness be not discovered thereon'; the prohibition, 'Neither shalt thou go up by steps unto mine altar,' that the altar should be low, or approached by a gentle slope, not by high steps; the reason, 'that thy nakedness be not discovered thereon,' that, in the loose garments then worn, going up by steps might immodestly expose the person, an unseemly thing in worship, afterward also guarded by the linen breeches of the priests; the great care of God for decency and reverence in his worship, that nothing unseemly or indecent should be done before him; the lesson the commentators draw, that God is to be worshipped with reverence, modesty, and order, all things done decently and in order; that even in such small matters God teaches the reverence due to his holiness).

themes: Neither shalt thou go up by steps unto mine altar
that thy nakedness be not discovered thereon
God forbidding steps up to his altar, guarding decency and modesty in worship
God to be worshipped with reverence and order, teaching the reverence due to his holiness
